cricket-odds-ai: A bottom-up TensorFlow model trained on 1M+ individual deliveries predicts ball outcomes rather than match winners.
The neural network outputs probabilities for 9 outcomes per ball (0–6, Wide, Wicket) using match state and player history as inputs.
A Monte Carlo MatchSimulator replays games thousands of times to produce a statistical distribution of likely outcomes.
The model outperformed professional bookmaker odds on 1,126 historical matches — bookmakers were less predictive than a zero-information baseline.
art-of-the-kill: Volume of shipped features is a vanity metric — the best PMs are defined by what they have the courage to kill.
Every feature must fight to exist; complexity is the silent killer of great UX.
"It's on the roadmap" and "Leadership asked for it" are echoes, not reasons — a roadmap is a hypothesis, not a mandate.
If you can't describe the human moment of relief or efficiency a feature creates, it doesn't have a soul.
gacha-trap: Whales — just 1–2% of players — generate 50–70% of Gacha game revenue, making psychological manipulation highly profitable.
Gacha mechanics mirror gambling so closely that Belgium, the Netherlands, and Japan have banned certain implementations.
Sunk cost fallacy, artificial difficulty walls, and FOMO-driven social proof are the core psychological levers developers pull.
The Pity System offers protection but doubles as an incentive to keep spending just a little more to reach the guaranteed reward.
stop-being-helpful: The most valuable employees aren't the most obedient — they're the ones who convince their managers to do things differently.
"Yes-Boss" employees are reliable but fragile; the team's success depends entirely on the manager never being wrong.
The top tier — "Jedis" — pushes back on bad plans, proposes smarter alternatives, and takes initiative beyond their brief.
Hierarchy is recursively flawed: domain expertise doesn't respect org-chart levels, and ego-driven deference costs companies dearly.
acquire-churn-repeat: Treating customer acquisition as your only growth lever is a structural flaw, not just a strategic preference.
Rising CAC means many brands lose money on the first transaction — without a second sale, every win is a deficit.
LTV, Repeat Purchase Rate, and LTV:CAC ratio are the metrics that matter; new customer count is a vanity metric.
Neglecting existing customers kills your referral engine and forces you back into the expensive ad market.
post-code-era: AI handles syntax; humans still own strategy, architecture, and judgment.
The old 1 PM + 1 Designer + 5–8 Engineers squad ratio no longer makes sense.
The new gold standard is a three-person team: Visionary PM, High-Taste Designer, Technical Architect.
Strategy and the ability to say 'No' are now the scarcest resources — not engineering capacity.
pickem: Led a 6-month end-to-end redesign of a B2B sportsbook platform used by 12 operators across Europe and LATAM.
Discovery included 12 operator interviews, uncovering 3 critical usability gaps in the existing product.
Delivered across 3 phased releases in 2-week sprints, collaborating with an engineering team of 8.
Reduced operator onboarding time by 40% and improved NPS from 31 to 58 post-launch.
reveals: Conceived and delivered a free-to-play daily engagement product for sportsbooks built on Gacha mechanics and an AI personalisation loop — in under six months.
The insight came from outside iGaming: mobile gaming had cracked habit formation, and those mechanics had never been applied to sports betting.
A Gemini Pro agent dynamically selects which players each user sees, balancing engagement likelihood against operator-defined KPIs and financial guardrails.
First customer going live. Targets: 20–30% DAU uplift and 15–25% reduction in first-session churn within 90 days.
claude-job-finder: A Python scraper built with Claude checks 7 iGaming job boards daily and generates a formatted PDF of new, relevant roles.
Deduplication means only genuinely new listings appear each day — no re-reviewing roles already seen.
A feedback layer lets the user mark roles as relevant or not, sharpening the output over time.
The whole thing was built in an afternoon. The real insight: most tedious recurring tasks can be automated if you just try.
predict-6: A free-to-play weekly score predictor — the first white-label equivalent of Sky Super 6 and BetVictor's 6 Goals.
Built to acquire the hesitant majority: users interested in sport but not yet ready to deposit real money.
The upsell funnel is placed after submission, not before — converting free players into real-money accumulators without breaking the zero-friction promise.
A £250,000 jackpot underwritten by an insurance broker makes the product revenue-neutral as an acquisition tool.
